cases.title: Beds Without Nurses
cases.presentation: During a regional mass-casualty event, a hospital administrator proudly reports 60 newly opened surge beds in a converted conference center. But the nursing supervisor notes there are no additional nurses, the supply room is nearly empty of monitors, and there is no patient-tracking process for the new area.
cases.question: What is the core problem?
options: A: There is no problem — the beds solve the surge.
B: Only one of the four S's (Space) was addressed; without Staff, Stuff, and Systems, real surge capacity has not increased.
C: The hospital should open even more beds.
D: Crisis standards of care should be declared immediately.
cases.explanation: Surge capacity requires all four S's — Staff, Stuff, Space, Systems. Sixty beds without nurses, monitors, or a tracking system is empty capacity. Command must balance the pillars, not just add space.
cases.clinicalPearl: Counting beds is the easiest and most misleading surge metric — capacity is set by your scarcest S.

cases.title: The Home on the Evacuation List
cases.presentation: A wildfire evacuation is ordered. On one street live: a man on home dialysis, an elderly woman who is deaf, a wheelchair-dependent veteran with no car, and a family whose child has autism and needs constant supervision. The standard evacuation notice was a single loud siren and an English-language text alert.
cases.question: Which framework best guides planning for these residents, and what does it reveal?
options: A: Triage colors — tag each red/yellow/green.

B: CMIST — Communication (deaf resident, non-English family), Maintaining health (dialysis), Independence (wheelchair), Support/Safety (child), Transportation (no car) — the siren-and-text plan fails all of them.
C: The four S's of surge.
cases.explanation: CMIST organizes access and functional needs: the residents need accessible communication, power/treatment continuity, mobility and transportation assistance, and supervision support. A single audio siren and English text alert reaches none of them — planning to CMIST closes those gaps.
cases.clinicalPearl: An evacuation plan that assumes everyone can hear, drive, walk, and self-manage leaves the most vulnerable behind — plan to CMIST.

cases.title: The Exhausted Crew
cases.presentation: Three weeks into a prolonged disaster response, a paramedic supervisor notices her crews are short-tempered, making uncharacteristic errors, sleeping poorly, and one medic quietly says the work feels 'pointless now.' Physical resources are adequate.
cases.question: What is happening and what should the supervisor do?
options: A: Ignore it — responders should be tough.
B: Recognize cumulative stress/burnout and moral distress; implement work-rest cycles, peer/buddy support, leadership check-ins, and access to behavioral-health follow-up.
C: Discipline the medic for negativity.
D: Add more shifts to catch up.
cases.explanation: Cumulative stress, moral distress, and burnout degrade judgment, safety, and health over a long response. Resilience measures — managed work-rest cycles, buddy systems, supportive leadership, and behavioral-health follow-up — protect the workforce, which is itself a critical resource. Caring for caregivers is part of the recovery link.
cases.clinicalPearl: The responders are part of the casualty count over a long event — protecting their minds sustains the whole response.
